voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Australia and Tasmania — eucalypt forests and coastal heath
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its large ears can swivel independently to listen in two directions at once.
Its eyes are set wide on its head for a nearly all-around view.
Its nose detects the scent of predators such as dingoes.
Its feet feel vibrations in the ground from approaching animals.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in eucalypt forest with open grassy clearings nearby.
Forest cover provides shade and shelter during the heat of the day.
Open grassy areas give it places to graze at dawn and dusk.
It can also be found in coastal heath and scrubland.
How does it send messages?: It thumps its hind foot loudly on the ground to warn nearby wallabies.
Males make a clucking sound during courtship to approach females.
It uses scent glands on its chest to mark resting spots.
A fast, heavy hop signals alarm and causes others to scatter.
What does it do when something changes?: It rests quietly in dense cover during the middle of the day.
If approached, it hops quickly into thick vegetation.
It may stand upright to get a better view of an unfamiliar sound.
When threatened, it cools itself by licking its forearms.
How does it learn?: A joey stays in the mother's pouch and travels with her for months.
It watches and smells what the mother eats before it grazes alone.
Young wallabies learn the edges of safe territory by following adults.
How does it have babies?: A tiny, underdeveloped joey is born and crawls into the mother's pouch.
The joey stays in the pouch, nursing and growing, for about nine months.
The mother can pause an embryo's growth until the pouch is empty.
After leaving the pouch, the young wallaby still returns to nurse.
What are its limits?: Hot midday temperatures force it to rest in shade and limit feeding.
Removal of forest cover leaves it exposed and without escape routes.
Dogs and foxes are major threats, especially to young joeys on the ground.
It needs both open grass and dense cover to survive.
What does this teach us?: Its pouch carries and protects young in a way few other animals can.
Its ears, eyes, and legs all work together to detect danger fast.
Its daily routine is timed around temperature, not just hunger.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Has embryonic diapause: the female can pause development until her pouch is empty.
Spends the hottest hours resting in dense cover and feeds at dawn and dusk.
The joey is born extremely underdeveloped and completes development in the pouch.
Native to Tasmania and mainland Australia in eucalypt forest and coastal heath.
still_debated: How much of the thump-warning is aimed at kin vs. all nearby wallabies.
How flexibly the species adapts to fragmented, human-modified landscapes.
Whether forearm-licking is primarily thermoregulation or has a signaling role.
common_failure_modes: Using 'kangaroo' interchangeably — Bennett's is a wallaby, not a kangaroo.
Describing the joey leaving the pouch as 'independent' — it returns to nurse for months.
Assuming all Australian marsupials share this species' ecology.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not call it a kangaroo.
Do not describe a fixed breeding season; diapause shifts timing.
Do not treat predator threats as uniform — dingoes, foxes, and dogs affect it differently.
